sentences.text: Yes, my name is Charlotte Melkman, M-E-L-K-M-A-N.
Blokjesman, B-L-O-K-J-E-S-M-A-N.
No.
It's either Charlotte or Lottie.
The 13th of April, 1920.
Nearly 75.
In Paisley, in Scotland.
P-A-I-S-L-E-Y.
Yes.
Yes, from about my third year, although I can't remember that, of course.
My parents used to live in Scotland, as you understand.
And when I was about three years, they went back to Holland, to Amsterdam.
About that time, I would say, yes.
I had two brothers.
Well, one was 16 when he was taken away.
And the other one was about two years younger than I, who was with me.
And I think again, we'll talk to that-- about that later.
Yes.
My grandmother did.
Well, there's not-- they used to live in Belgium.
They only used to come over on holidays.
And we used to go on holidays to stay with her.
They came back to live in Holland, and my grandfather died.
And she came to live with us.
That's all I can say.
Lovely, lovely woman.
She was tiny, reddish hair.
You know, lovely like any grandmother.
Yes.
Oh, no.
No.
Not too close, good-- good understanding.
Yes.
No, a house.
[SIGHS] How would I say?
My father had business at the house.
So it was always-- customers used to come and used to come through the living room to-- I suppose-- he was an agency for British houses, and rain coats, and rubber boots and so on.
So there was always activity in our house, and especially on rainy days, you know, he was very busy.
And the living room was-- on the big table, they-- they used to make parcels, and trucks used to come to deliver and pick up.
You know, so it's--
--everything in the house, kind of.
Was involved with the business.
Yes.
No, not really, no.
And what-- he would import goods from England.
Yes, yes.
And he got it on consignment.
And once a year, he used to go to England and Scotland.
Later on, he also had houses in Canada, for [INAUDIBLE] boots, rubber boots.
He used to--
The British-- Netherlands British Agency, NBA.
Yes, she used to do the typing, and when father wasn't home, she used to attend to the customers.
So she was also quite involved in it.
Actually, I must tell you this.
There was one customer who used to come.
And during the war, at the beginning of the war, my father used to sell sails cloth to him.

And not until a couple of years ago, I found out that that man who bought the sails cloth, was actually rebuilding fishing trollies into sea-faring boats who were going to-- trying to get over to England during the war.
So whether my father knew it or not, he was involved a little bit in underground work.
And I only found it out a couple of years ago.
That's a very long story.
But I was with a friend in Holland.
And he got a, a newspaper from the underground called 1940-45.
And he said, hey Lottie, he said, did your father sell sails cloth?
That was somebody writing the paper, that he was looking for people who used to-- he was trying to find out how many people reached England in those little boats.
And then the name of a Mr.
Birens ?] was mentioned.
I said, I know Mr.
Birens ?].
He used to come to our house.
He was a customer.
And so it proved that my father sold sails cloth to him, which he used to make little fishing boats sea-worthy.
OK, but your father may or may not have been aware of what it was for.
I don't know whether he know it and kept silent from myself.
I, I don't know.
I'll never know.
Yes.
No.
But we knew we were Jewish.
We had a bit of Jewish education.
We knew all about it, traditional.
On Sunday, we had special lessons.
My father got a teacher in to, to give us lessons at home.
Well, we knew when the holidays were, like Passover, you know.
We had the, the Jewish Pass-- you know, matzah.
And-- but not really.
Friday night was a little bit extra.
Mother used to do extra cooking.
Two-- she had two stoves going in the kitchen on a Friday.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, you know, chicken, chicken soup, vegetables, you know.
A little bit extra than during the week.
And lots of customers, you know, I knew that when they came on a Friday, there was always something for them.
[LAUGHS] My mother always served them a plate of soup or something.
Normal, not rich, not poor, very normal.
When it was a very rainy season, my father made, you know, money.
And sometimes he-- you know, not regular income.
So it really just depended on, on the weather.
On the weather, yeah, more or less.
Rainy season was good, yeah.
Yeah.
No, but we-- whatever we needed, we got, you know.
We never went short of anything.
Yes.
Not really.
Not very well, because they didn't make such a big business out of it as they do these days.
These days, they get cars and overseas trips, and so on.
I remember when-- [LAUGHS] in those days, boys went-- when they became bar mitzvahed, they got a first watch.
That was a bar mitzvah present.
Now, they're born with watches.
It's different.
No, not really, no.
Very vague.
Nothing really.
Not like here, that, you know, you volunteer for this, or volunteer for that.
No, we didn't.
I don't know, we just lived better, because there were so many Jews living in-- in Amsterdam, especially, that we never gave it a thought, really.
No, we just accepted that, and that was it.
We were Jewish, and that was it.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
As I said, you know, customers came.
Even at nighttime they used to come, and come in the house, have a cup of tea, you know, sit at the table.
[LAUGHS]
No, No.
No.
Never gave it a thought.
Didn't exist, you know?
It's--
Oh, yes.
Oh, when she came to visit Amsterdam, you know, we used to go out and stand in the street and wave.
Yes.
Talking about the Queen, I read the other day, that the Crown Prince of Holland is probably marrying an Israeli girl.
So that's the royal family in Holland.
Oh, yes, very much.
Yeah.
Yeah, very much they were for the Jews, the House of Orange, this, the royal family.
Yes.
[SIGHS]
Both.
Both.
It really just was an issue at all.
No, no.
I can't remember.
[LAUGHS] Doing my homework.
I used to go for a while to table tennis.
And afterwards when we were not allowed to go anywhere, they had special Jewish clubs.
And used to go to tennis on Saturday afternoon.
That was already when the Germans were there.
And also used to go to a, a sewing evening to learn to sew, also from the Jewish authorities, because that was all we were allowed.
Yes, at the corner of our street, there was a Russian synagogue.
And right next to us, as next door neighbors in the backyard, there was a little synagogue, Portuguese.
Of course, there were lots of Portuguese, Spanish Portuguese people in Amsterdam.

And when they used to go to that synagogue, they had to go through the front door of those people, through the long corridor, part of the kitchen, and so to the-- to the synagogue in the backyard-- and-- just over the fence.
Yeah.
I used to go-- I loved to go there on a Thursday and polished all the copper.
I didn't have to.
But, you know, I liked to do that.
It was just something that you volunteered to do.
Yeah.
Yeah, used to help.
Oh no.
Ah, well, there's always lots of copper in a synagogue like that.
Candles, candelabras-- ornaments.
No, not ornaments, but decorative.
When I was 15, after-- leaving examination.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
It was '35 or-- '35, I think, 1935.
I worked as a assistant cashier in a big store, something like compared to Woolworth's.
And I worked as an assistant cashier, and [INAUDIBLE].
HEMA.
H-E-M-A.
Loved it, yes.
I don't know.
Because I had-- there were lots and lots of sales girls.
And every department had a cash register.
And I-- at night time, you know, I had to take the-- see how much everyone made.
And in the morning, the girls used to come.
And I used to have to count money, and make up the lists, and send to the headquarters.
And, you know, I was a lot involved.
I liked this.
Well, I had a special girlfriend.
We used to go out on a Saturday night, Sunday afternoon.
Yes, she was Jewish, yes.
We'd just go to town, walk main streets, have a cup of coffee.
It was beautiful, beautiful country, free.
Although we didn't-- we just took it for granted.
It's a beautiful country.
Looking at the windows, window shopping, you know, off of the main streets.
I don't know.
I can't remember really.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, but we didn't pay much attention, you know.
It's far away.
You know, that's what-- although Holland did a lot in 1936.
They took lots of German refugees in.
And that's when they built the camp where we went later on, Westerbork.
But not with barracks.
They had houses.
And, you know, people got houses, those German refugees.
Well, we had some friends.
As a matter of fact, that man used to be a film photographer for the UFA in Germany.
And he made lovely photos of us always.
And--
UFA, it's the German-- like MGM in, you know, different-- films, big German film studio at the time.
And they were lovely people.
We were-- became very friendly with them.
They went to America later.
And they--
No.
No.
I was too young to pay any attention to that.
That all went, you know, past me.
I, I didn't pay much-- you know, as a young girl-- not like these days with all the television and so on.
You don't pay much attention to it because it didn't, didn't concern you.
You know?
It's--
No, no.
No, not in Holland.
No.
When?
No, I wasn't aware of anything that-- I'm probably not very helpful in that, because, you know, I was a young girl.
You were not told much, and didn't think twice.
Well, in the beginning, the first month, nothing.
Everything went as normal.
But then gradually, I had to register.
We had to wear the star.
We were not allowed in the trains, in the buses, not allowed in certain shops.
There were shops only for Jews, and shops no Jews allowed.
Not in parks, not travelling.
You had to get a special pass if you wanted to travel.
In by 8 o'clock at night, like a curfew.
And, you know, it became worse and worse.
No, but I woke up in the morning and grandmother-- the Germans have invaded.
You have to be careful.
Now we can't talk.
And you have to be very careful and, and the bombardment of Rotterdam you heard on the radio.
And it was very bad in Rotterdam.
And--
Well, not-- me, not personal.
Because, you know, as I said, I never paid much attention.
But I think my parents did.
[INAUDIBLE] May, 1940.
I then said to my father, look.
Lots of people trying to get to England.
Why don't we [INAUDIBLE]?
It's not none of your business.
Keep out of it.
That's what the answer, you know.
It was not up to the children.
They were seen-- to be seen and not heard.
Father knows best, and he didn't.
No, because there was not much opportunity.
It was only a few days, and then, you know, after they invaded, and Holland capitulated, it was finished.
So you couldn't get out then anyway.
No, and there was nothing we could flee to because, you know, the border was Germany.
Belgium was also occupied.
All we could do was [INAUDIBLE] to see, through the worst.
So when you actually had to wear the stars--
Yeah?
Well, they had a big strike.
Everybody stopped work.
The trains-- the buses, the trains, everything, one day, and then just-- the Germans just picked up a lot of people, and shot them, and it was finished.
And it's still remembered every year as the February Strike.
They've got a big statue in, in the city.
And it's still remembered.
See.
They, they wanted to help, but they couldn't do much.
Yes.
And, you know, everybody says, oh, the Dutch were so good, you know.
For every good one, there were two bad ones, or maybe the other way around.
But there were a lot of bad ones.
And very, very many very good ones.

Well, that they would place-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were-- as soon as people were taken out of their houses to be sent to the camps, there used to come a big furniture van, Dutch people, and emptied the house, went to Germany.
Yes, yes.
Bad, very bad.
Because that was the reason why my fiance was picked up.
There was a big furniture van opposite this.
And he came to say goodbye to me, because he went to a hiding place.
And he didn't wear a star.
And I wasn't home.
And when they saw me coming in the street, he came up to me.
I wore a star.
He didn't, and those Dutch people from the furniture van picked him out.
And that's how he was caught.
No, no.
Not very long.
I only met him in June, I think, and in September he went.
But in the short time, we met for such a lot, it, it was like years.
Just in the street.
Because I went out with some girlfriends, and wearing the star.
And he was by himself.
And he started talking to us with-- normally.
You know, you don't answer.
You don't go into that.
But seeing he had a star, you know, it attracted those days.
And he took us to an ice cream parlor.
And then we took the-- my girlfriends home, and he took me home.
And that's how it started.
Normally, you know, I wouldn't pick up anyone from the street.
I wouldn't have done that.
But this time it was different, of course.
So everything was very intense at that time.
Yeah, and as it had to be, I had neighbors.
And his uncle, I'll tell you later about him, he was married to a lady who used to live next door.
And after the first night when he-- when I met him, he went to them and made inquiries about me.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
OK.
He never proposed.
It all went automatically really.
[LAUGHS]
No, no, we just went.
And when I got my calling-up papers, and I thought I had to go.
And we just bought a ring, and we were engaged.
And that was it.
And then I got a fortnight to wait before I went.
And it became months.
Because of my British-- I had British papers.
Well, first of all, when they were picked up from their homes, or when they had voluntary-- you know, when they got a call up and just had to go, we were going to a theatre, and assembled there.
From there, the Germans worked out who had to go and who didn't have to go.
And then they were sent to Westerbork.
And from there, every Tuesday, the train would come.
And in the middle of the night, the lights went on, and the lists were read out.
And people whose name was called out started packing again.
And the next morning after, went in the cattle trucks.
Yes, one day, two days, or sometimes a few hours.
My parents apparently were in there only couple of hours.
No, I was picked-- picked up from home by the [INAUDIBLE].
What do you mean?
Well, they just came in the house.
And we just had to go.
I said, yeah, but I'm British.
You know, I don't have to go.
They were Germans.
And they said, oh, you know, work that out later.
You have to come now.
So I went to Bergen-Belsen.
No, first to Westerbork.
But if we can just talk about-- you were taken to the place where everybody was collected together.
And then--
No, we were taken to Westerbork straight away.
They were gone already.
I was the last one in the house.
My family was taken.
My parents were just-- there was a ring at the door.
And there were the, the Dutch police picked them up.
They just had to go.
That was it.
And at-- we had very good friends.
She was our housemate.
And her husband tried everything the next day to try and do something for them.
But it was too late.
They had gone already.
And these friends were non-Jewish.
They were non-Jewish, yes.
What do you-- to whom?
Oh, well, yes.
No.
No.
No.
But my fiance was picked up, like, a few days before them, before my parents.
And he was taking to Briesen first.
And they met up in the camp in Westerbork .
And they went in the same train.
He traveled with them until a certain town in Germany.
And then the elderly had to get out.
So my parents went out, and he stayed in.
And that was it.
Yeah, and I met his parents in Westerbork.
Well, my brother, the youngest one, he was 16.
He got-- the three of us got call-up papers.
But my brother and myself got out of it for the time being because of British-- because we were born in Britain.
And the young brother just went.
He had to assemble at such-and-such a time, such-and-such a spot.
And he just went.
It will be all right.
If I don't go, they'll take the parents.
That's what everybody said.
So he felt that if he didn't-- if didn't go, then he would be putting other people in danger.
Lots of people did not go.
They went into hiding.
But--
Yes.
See, the administration in Holland was so good, they had everything on paper.
They're good.
Not like in Denmark.
